text: The surgeon uses Maryland dissection forceps to lift the gallbladder and liver, preparing to introduce a fundal grasper from the right side of the patient.

The surgeon uses a fundal grasper to catch the head of the gallbladder and push it back, creating space for dissection. The thick gallbladder presents some difficulty, but it is successfully pushed back against the liver. Carbon dioxide is added to inflate the space, separating nearby organs. A Hartmann's pouch forceps is introduced to grasp the Hartmann's pouch.

The surgeon uses forceps to carefully peel off the transparent peritoneum layer from the gallbladder, revealing underlying blood vessels. The duodenum, the first part of the small intestine, is visible falling down as the dissection progresses. The surgeon identifies and separates fatty planes between the gallbladder wall and the mesothelial peritoneum.

The surgeon gently lifts and tears the peritoneum layer away from the gallbladder, revealing the organ as it emerges from the surrounding tissue. The gallbladder is visibly supplied with blood, indicating its living state.

The surgeon encounters bleeding from torn capillaries during dissection, visible in the magnified laparoscopic view. A larger vessel is pulled, causing bleeding, while the surgical field is displayed at eight times magnification on the monitor.

The surgeon performs posterior dissection within the classical Calot's triangle, carefully separating small vessels, fibrous tissue, and fatty material between the cystic duct and gallbladder using laparoscopic instruments.

The surgeon introduces a Maryland dissector into the Calot's triangle, working between the cystic artery and cystic duct. The flimsy peritoneum allows visibility of the common bile duct and common hepatic ducts below. The cystic duct appears elongated. The surgeon gently peels off fibro-fatty tissue, revealing small veins and a glimpse of a lymph node near the cystic artery, along with visible pulsations of the artery.
The surgeon carefully manipulates the tissue around the cystic artery to avoid tearing it, while addressing significant bleeding from the cystic vein by cauterizing it.

The surgeon uses monopolar cautery to ensure hemostasis, carefully pulling tissue away from main arteries and ducts to prevent conduction. The artery is visible, and the surgeon introduces a curved instrument to manipulate a lymph node, which is lifted without removal. The dissection progresses above the junction between the gallbladder and liver bed, separating these structures.

The surgeon performs a large dissection, clearly identifying the cystic duct entering the gallbladder and the cystic artery. A vein is cauterized to prevent bleeding, and the surgeon pulls it apart. Additional minor bleeding is cauterized to maintain a clear surgical field.

The surgeon cautiously uses monopolar cautery away from the common hepatic duct and common bile duct to avoid injury. A small vessel, identified as the posterior branch of the cystic artery, is dissected and clipped to prevent bleeding.

The surgeon places a clip on a small vessel near the cystic duct and artery, assessing whether to clip or cauterize the posterior branch of the cystic artery. The dissection reveals the cystic duct, cystic artery, and a posterior branch, with the anatomy clearly demonstrated for identification.

The surgeon uses a clip applicator to apply the first clip onto the cystic duct, positioning it away from the junction with the common hepatic and common bile ducts to avoid compromising these important structures.

The surgeon applies a distal clip onto the cystic duct, which connects the gallbladder to the bile ducts leading to the duodenum. Three clips are securely placed on the duct. The surgeon then applies a proximal clip onto the cystic artery, ensuring it is positioned away from the common hepatic duct.

The surgeon loads and applies a second clip onto the cystic duct, ensuring extra precaution by using multiple clips. Two clips are placed on the cystic duct and two on the proximal side. The surgeon then cuts between the two proximal clips, severing the cystic duct. The cystic duct and cystic artery are visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon uses a monopolar cautery hook to control small bleeding from the distal part of the cystic artery. The gallbladder is being carefully mobilized and separated from the liver by introducing the hook's tip under the connective tissue, tearing it apart gently through the proper anatomical plane to avoid injury and prevent leakage of gallbladder contents into the abdominal cavity.

The surgeon addresses bleeding from dilated veins near the liver surface, applying cautery and pressure with gauze to control it. The gallbladder is being peeled away from the liver bed, using cauterization and gentle tearing to separate the tissue along a visible plane.
